overview.marketStructure: Oligopoly - Top 6 companies control ~68% of organized market share. Tier-1 (TCS, Infosys) dominate with 35%, Tier-2 (Wipro, HCL, Tech M) hold 25%, rest is fragmented.
demandDrivers: Digital transformation initiatives across BFSI, Retail, Manufacturing sectors
Cloud migration and modernization projects (AWS, Azure, GCP adoption)
AI/ML implementation and data analytics requirements
5G rollout creating demand for telecom IT services
Regulatory compliance (GDPR, data localization) driving demand

keyRawMaterials.material: Skilled IT Workforce (Software Engineers, Architects, Project Managers)
keyRawMaterials.dependency: Domestic supply (85%) from IITs, NITs, tier-2 engineering colleges. International hiring (15%) impacted by H1B visa policies.
Mix of owned infrastructure (40%), leased data centers (30%), public cloud (AWS/Azure/GCP - 30%)
keyRawMaterials.supplyRisk: Low - Multiple providers, scalable on demand

overview.entryBarriers: High - Requires (1) Scale to serve large enterprises, (2) Proven delivery track record, (3) Domain expertise, (4) Global delivery model with onsite-offshore mix, (5) Certifications (CMMI Level 5, ISO), (6) Significant upfront investment in sales and delivery infrastructure
overview.regulatoryEnvironment: Moderately regulated. Key factors: (1) H1B visa policies impact onsite staffing, (2) Data privacy laws (GDPR, CCPA) require compliance investments, (3) Tax incentives via SEZ/STPI benefits, (4) Work-from-home policies post-COVID creating cross-border taxation issues
overview.cyclicality: Low to Moderate - Defensive sector but exposed to client IT spending cycles. Discretionary projects (new implementations) are cyclical, while maintenance contracts provide stability. ~60% revenue from annuity contracts reduces cyclicality.

threatOfNewEntrants.reasoning: High entry barriers due to scale requirements, client relationships, and delivery track record needed

bargainingPowerOfSuppliers.reasoning: Tech talent in demand leads to wage inflation. Cloud providers have some pricing power.

bargainingPowerOfBuyers.reasoning: Large enterprise clients negotiate aggressively. Switching costs exist but buyers demand price reductions annually.

threatOfSubstitutes.reasoning: Automation, low-code platforms, and client captive centers are substitutes. Increasing use of AI tools.

competitiveRivalry.reasoning: Intense competition among Indian IT firms and global competitors (Accenture, Cognizant). Price-based competition common.

emergingTrends: GenAI adoption: Companies building AI practices, but also risk of AI reducing billable hours
Platform-based revenue models replacing traditional time-and-materials billing
Nearshoring trend: Clients preferring Mexico/Eastern Europe over India for some projects
Automation paradox: Improves margins but reduces headcount-based revenue growth
Cloud hyperscalers competing directly for large transformation deals
innovationsDisruptions: Generative AI could reduce coding effort by 30-40%, impacting utilization rates
Client captive centers growing, taking away outsourcing demand
Open-source alternatives reducing license revenue for product companies
